facts_disclosed: Book project scrapped — realized a documentary would reach more people

turns.content: so I have made a decision and I need to talk it through before I lose my nerve. can I think out loud with you for a minute
Of course — go ahead. What's on your mind?

I had been working on a book project — a book about the architectural heritage of Palestinian families in Chile, how they built homes here that carry echoes of the places they were forced to leave. I had chapters outlined, research gathered, interviews recorded. it was going to be my love letter to the diaspora in built form
That sounds like a deeply meaningful project. What's the decision you've made?

the book is scrapped. I am done with it. finished. I have been wrestling with this for weeks and I finally admitted to myself what I think I knew all along — I was trying to describe walls and doorways and the way light falls through a courtyard in words, when a camera can show you that truth in a single frame. the book is dead
That's a big shift. It sounds like you've been sitting with this for a while though — not a snap decision. What made it crystallize?

a few things came together. I was writing a chapter about the Haddad family house in Recoleta — this incredible structure with arched windows that look like they belong in Beit Jala, not Santiago — and I spent three pages describing the stonework around the entrance. three pages. and then I showed a photograph of the same entrance to a friend and she understood in two seconds what I had been struggling to put into words for a week. that was the moment. forget about the book
A photograph capturing in an instant what three pages couldn't — that's a pretty clear sign. So are you moving to a visual medium? Photography, film?

a documentary. I have a filmmaker friend, Javiera, who has been telling me for months that this material belongs on screen. she was right and I was too stubborn to listen. we are going to make it together — she handles the camera and the storytelling structure, I handle the architectural knowledge and the community connections. the book is off the table entirely, it is not happening. we are doing this with images and voices and the sound of hands on stone

That partnership sounds really strong — you bring the subject matter expertise and community trust, she brings the filmmaking craft. A documentary about diaspora architecture has the potential to reach a much wider audience than a specialized book, too. Have you started planning the structure?

we have had two long conversations about it. Javiera wants to follow three or four families — film their homes here in Chile and then, inshallah, eventually travel to Palestine and film the original houses or what remains of them. the before and after of exile, told through architecture. she calls it a love story between two geographies. I almost cried when she said that

A love story between two geographies — that's a stunning framing. The visual parallel of filming the Chilean homes alongside the Palestinian originals could be incredibly powerful. Do you have a sense of the timeline for production?

we want to start filming here in Santiago before the end of the year — the Chilean houses first, because those are accessible and I already have relationships with the families. the Palestine footage would come later, maybe next year, depending on funding and access. that is the other question — funding. we need to apply for grants

Starting with Santiago footage is smart — it gives you a proof of concept and trailer material for grant applications. For funding, a few avenues:

- **FONDART audiovisual fund** — they have a specific category for documentary.
- **Arab Fund for Arts and Culture (AFAC)** — perfect fit for a diaspora cultural project.
- **Sundance Documentary Fund** — competitive but they actively seek international stories.
- **International documentary co-production funds** — having a Chilean-Palestinian angle makes you eligible for several cross-cultural funding streams.

A three-minute trailer cut from the Santiago footage would be your strongest tool for all of these applications.

AFAC — I had not thought of them, that is an excellent suggestion. and the trailer strategy is exactly what Javiera proposed. she is very practical, which is good because I am the one who writes three pages about a doorway and then throws the whole book away

Ha — but that instinct for deep observation is exactly what will make you invaluable to this documentary. You see things in buildings that other people walk past. The difference now is you'll have a camera to capture what your eye finds instead of trying to translate it into paragraphs. Do you have any concerns about the switch?
my main concern is that I wasted months on the book — the research, the chapter drafts, the outlines. months of work sitting in a folder on my computer, useless

I'd push back on that. All of those chapters, research notes, and interview transcripts become the intellectual backbone of the documentary — your shot list, your interview questions, your narration material. The research doesn't disappear just because the format changes. You're not starting from zero.

Diego said the same thing. he said no work is wasted, that the book was the foundation and the documentary is the building that rises from it. he is probably right. he usually is, which is infuriating. but no, I am at peace with this. the book was the scaffolding — now we take it down and let the real structure show
The scaffolding metaphor is perfect — especially coming from an architect. And Diego's right. When do you and Javiera meet next to start planning in detail?

next week. she wants to scout locations — start with the Haddad house since that is the one I know best and the family has already said yes to being part of the project. we will walk through the house together and she will see what I have been trying to describe in those useless three pages. except now the house will speak for itself

The Haddad house is a perfect starting point — you already have the relationship, the research, and the emotional connection. And the house will absolutely speak for itself through Javiera's lens. Good luck with the scouting next week.

thank you. yalla, time to learn how to be on the other side of a camera. a terrifying thought for someone who prefers to stand behind measuring tapes and blueprints. but Neruda said something about being afraid of the whole world and doing it anyway. I think that applies here
Neruda would approve. And the documentary world needs people who care this deeply about their subject. Go build something new — in a different medium this time.
